title: On War
excerpt: The foundational philosophical treatise on the nature of war, introducing concepts like 'friction,' 'the fog of war,' 'center of gravity,' and war as the continuation of politics by other means.
keyIdeas: War is the continuation of politics by other means — it is fundamentally political, not an independent activity
The 'remarkable trinity' of war: primordial violence, chance and probability, and rational political purpose
Friction makes even the simplest thing difficult in war — the gap between plans and reality is inevitable
The center of gravity: the hub of all power and movement upon which everything depends
keyTakeaways: War cannot be understood in isolation from its political context; military objectives serve political ends
Perfect knowledge is impossible in war — commanders must act under uncertainty, which is the normal condition
Theoretical frameworks are useful but must be adapted to the specific situation; war resists theoretical reduction
Victory requires identifying and striking the enemy's center of gravity while protecting your own
whoShouldRead: Military professionals and strategists — the foundational text of Western military theory
Political scientists and historians studying the relationship between war and politics
Anyone seeking a philosophical understanding of war beyond tactics and strategy
whoShouldSkip: Readers looking for a practical how-to guide for winning battles — this is a philosophical treatise, not a manual
Those wanting a concise summary — the book is long, unfinished, and often difficult
Anyone expecting modern military doctrine — Clausewitz's examples are from the Napoleonic era
